form.buy: Plain **creatine monohydrate**. Micronised dissolves better but is chemically identical. Look for a **high-purity, third-party-tested** monohydrate — independent purity testing is worth a small premium.
form.avoid: ‘Advanced’ forms — HCl, ethyl ester, buffered (Kre-Alkalyn), nitrate, liquid. None beat monohydrate head-to-head, and they cost 3–10× more for the same or worse result.
form.withFood: Doesn't need food. Co-ingesting carbs/protein raises insulin, which nudges muscle uptake — but once you're saturated this barely matters.
form.bioavailability: Near-complete oral absorption at doses ≤5 g; essentially ~100% bioavailable as monohydrate.

biomarkers.why: Creatine pulls water into muscle. A +0.5–2 kg jump in the first 1–2 weeks confirms it's loading into the tissue.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then week 2

biomarkers.why: Rises modestly on creatine — a benign lab artifact from the extra substrate, **not** kidney damage. Know this before a routine panel so a high reading isn't misread.
biomarkers.when: Flag it before any eGFR/kidney test

needs.role: Insulin drives creatine into muscle via GLUT4; co-ingesting carbs raised uptake in early loading studies.

antagonists.role: Some evidence a large acute caffeine dose blunts creatine's ergogenic effect the same session — the interaction is small and debated, and daily coffee is fine.

foodFirst.line: ~450 g (1 lb) of **raw** red meat or fish supplies roughly **2 g** creatine — about half a daily dose. Cooking destroys 20–30% of it.
foodFirst.note: This is why vegetarians and vegans carry lower muscle creatine and tend to respond the strongest to supplementing.

cost.note: The cheapest evidence-backed supplement there is, per effective dose.

dosing.note: A flat **3–5 g/day** works for almost everyone — per-kg only matters at the extremes. Optional loading: 0.3 g/kg/day split into 4 doses for 5–7 days saturates faster, then drop to maintenance.

timing.line: Barely matters — it's a saturation game, not a pre-workout stopwatch. If you want the tiny edge, take it **post-workout with carbs/protein**. Consistency (daily) beats timing.

cycling.line: **No cycling needed.** No tolerance, no receptor downregulation, no washout. Take it daily, indefinitely. Stopping just lets muscle stores fade over ~4–6 weeks.

contra.advice: Monohydrate is safe for healthy kidneys, but data in established CKD is thin — clear it with a doctor first.

quality.line: Choose a **third-party-tested** monohydrate (independent certification such as Informed Sport or NSF). Cheap bulk creatine from poor synthesis can carry contaminants like dicyandiamide and excess creatinine — independent purity testing is the safeguard.

nonResponders.line: ~20–30% see little scale/strength jump — usually because their muscle creatine is **already near-full** from a meat-heavy diet, so there's no room to top up. Vegetarians almost always respond.

synergy.why: Creatine only expresses its benefit when you supply the training stimulus it fuels — it's a battery, not a spark.
The insulin bump drives more creatine into muscle during loading.

form.buy: **D3 (cholecalciferol)** — raises blood levels more effectively than D2. Choose an **oil-based softgel** (it's fat-soluble) and pair K2 as **MK-7** for its long half-life.
form.avoid: D2 (ergocalciferol) for daily use, and dry tablets taken on an empty stomach — both absorb poorly. Mega-'immune' megadoses without a blood test.
form.withFood: **Take with the day's fattiest meal** — fat-soluble vitamins need dietary fat present, which can raise absorption by ~30–50%.
form.bioavailability: Highly variable and food-dependent; absorption climbs substantially when taken with fat vs. fasted.

biomarkers.why: The single number that matters — it's your actual vitamin D status. Dose to the target, don't guess.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then ~8–12 weeks after a dose change; twice a year

biomarkers.why: The safety check. Sustained high-dose D can push calcium up (hypercalcemia) — the main real risk of over-supplementing.
biomarkers.when: If taking >4000 IU/day long-term

needs.role: A required cofactor for the enzymes that activate vitamin D. Low magnesium blunts your response no matter how much D you take.
Directs the calcium D absorbs **into bone** and away from arteries — which is why they're paired.

depletes.role: High-dose D increases demand for both — supplementing D alone can unmask a magnesium or K2 shortfall.

foodFirst.line: Fatty fish leads: **salmon ~400–600 IU per 100 g**, plus egg yolks and UV-grown mushrooms. But food rarely covers a deficit — **10–30 min of midday sun** makes far more, if your latitude and skin allow. For K2: **natto** is off the charts, then hard cheeses and egg yolk.
foodFirst.note: Above ~35° latitude you make almost no vitamin D from sun in winter — which is when supplementing matters most.

cost.note: Pennies a day to correct a genuine deficiency — one of the highest value-per-dollar supplements when you're actually low.

timing.line: **With your largest fatty meal**, any time of day. A few people report high evening doses disturb sleep (weak evidence) — if that's you, take it in the morning. D3's long half-life means a weekly dose works as well as daily.

cycling.line: No cycling. D3 stores in fat with a **~2–3 week half-life**, so daily or once-weekly dosing both work. Re-test and titrate to your 25-OH-D target rather than cycling.

contra.advice: These conditions dysregulate vitamin D activation and can cause hypercalcemia even at normal doses — supplement only under medical supervision.

contra.flag: Thiazide diuretics or a history of calcium kidney stones
contra.advice: Both raise hypercalcemia/stone risk when stacked with high-dose D — get calcium monitored.

quality.line: Choose **USP-verified** products. Independent testing has repeatedly found softgel D content ranging from ~9% to ~140% of the label. For K2, look for **all-trans MK-7**.

nonResponders.line: If your 25-OH-D won't budge, suspect: **obesity** (D sequesters in fat), **fat malabsorption** (celiac, IBD, bariatric surgery), or **low magnesium** (can't activate it). Genetic GC/VDR variants also shift where your set-point lands.

synergy.why: Routes the extra absorbed calcium into bone instead of soft tissue and arteries.
Powers the enzymes that convert D to its active form — the missing link in many non-responders.

form.buy: Read the label for **EPA + DHA content**, not total 'fish oil' mg. **Triglyceride (rTG/TG)** form absorbs better than ethyl ester; **phospholipid** (krill) is well-absorbed too. Enteric-coated caps cut the fishy burps.
form.avoid: Cheap **ethyl ester (EE)** concentrates taken fasted — absorption is markedly lower without food. And rancid, oxidised oil (see quality).
form.withFood: **Take with a fat-containing meal.** Absorption of EE form especially collapses when fasted and roughly triples with a high-fat meal.
form.bioavailability: Form- and meal-dependent: rTG > phospholipid ≈ free-fatty-acid > ethyl ester; all improve substantially taken with fat.

biomarkers.why: Red-blood-cell EPA+DHA — the validated status marker. It's the omega-3 equivalent of 25-OH-D.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then ~3–4 months later

biomarkers.why: The clearest dose-dependent effect — high-dose fish oil lowers triglycerides.
biomarkers.when: If taking it for lipids

needs.role: Polyunsaturated oils oxidise easily; a little vitamin E (many products add it) protects the oil and your tissue stores.

antagonists.role: Omega-6 and omega-3 compete for the same desaturase/elongase enzymes and eicosanoid pathways — a very high omega-6 intake works against your omega-3 status.

foodFirst.line: **Fatty fish twice a week** basically covers general needs — salmon, mackerel and sardines give ~1–2 g EPA+DHA per serving. Plant ALA (flax, chia, walnuts) is **not** a substitute: conversion is <10% to EPA and ~1% to DHA.
foodFirst.note: If you eat oily fish regularly you may not need a supplement at all — test your Omega-3 Index before buying.

cost.math: Cost varies with concentration — a concentrated rTG can be cheaper per gram of actual EPA+DHA than a bulk low-strength oil.
cost.note: Compare on cost-per-gram-EPA+DHA, not per capsule — low-strength oils hide the real price.

timing.line: **With a fatty meal**, and split larger doses across two meals to cut burps and steady absorption. Time of day doesn't matter for the benefit.

cycling.line: No cycling — it's a status you build and maintain, like vitamin D. Stores decline over months if you stop. Steady daily intake keeps the Omega-3 Index up.

contra.advice: High doses modestly affect bleeding time; the clinical bleeding risk is small but worth flagging to your doctor, especially before surgery.
Large trials (REDUCE-IT, STRENGTH) found a small increase in AF signal at **≥4 g/day** — relevant only at prescription-level doses.

quality.line: Oxidation is the real problem — a lot of retail fish oil is already **rancid**. Look for **IFOS certification** and low oxidation markers (TOTOX), plus heavy-metal and PCB testing. A fish oil that smells strongly fishy is oxidised.

nonResponders.line: Poor **ALA→EPA/DHA converters** (common, and worse with **FADS gene** variants) get little from plant sources and must use fish/algae oil directly. Women convert ALA somewhat better than men.

synergy.why: Protects the fragile PUFA from oxidising before and after you absorb it.
Dietary fat triggers the bile and lipase that carry omega-3s across the gut.

form.buy: **Glycinate (bisglycinate)** for sleep and calm, **citrate** if you also want mild bowel help, **malate** for daytime/muscle use.
form.avoid: **Magnesium oxide** — poorly absorbed and mostly acts as a laxative; also skip cheap 'oxide' blends dressed up as sleep aids.
form.withFood: Take with or without food; **glycinate is gentle** on an empty stomach, while citrate/oxide are better spread out to avoid loose stools.
form.bioavailability: Organic forms (**glycinate, citrate, malate**) absorb better than oxide; check the **elemental magnesium** figure, not the total salt weight.

biomarkers.why: The standard, cheap lab — but only ~1% of body magnesium is extracellular, so it stays 'normal' until you are quite depleted. Useful mainly to rule out overt deficiency.
biomarkers.when: Fasting; recheck only if symptomatic or on interacting meds

biomarkers.why: Reflects intracellular stores better than serum and is a more sensitive read of true status if you want a real baseline.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then after ~8-12 weeks of supplementing

needs.role: May support cellular magnesium uptake; the two are often paired for this reason, though the mechanistic evidence is limited.
Magnesium is a required cofactor for the enzymes that activate vitamin D — low magnesium blunts your response to D.

depletes.role: Increases urinary magnesium loss; heavy or chronic intake is a common driver of low status.
Mild diuretic effect raises renal magnesium excretion.

antagonists.role: Competes for intestinal absorption when taken together in large single doses — separate them.
Chronic proton-pump-inhibitor use is a recognised cause of low magnesium.

foodFirst.line: **Pumpkin seeds, almonds, leafy greens, legumes, whole grains and dark chocolate** are all magnesium-dense — a genuinely varied wholefood diet covers most people's baseline needs.
foodFirst.note: Food magnesium has no laxative ceiling; supplementation mainly matters for hard trainers, high stress, alcohol use, or documented low status.

cost.math: A bottle of ~100-120 glycinate capsules runs roughly SGD 15-35, i.e. cents per serving.
cost.note: One of the cheapest evidence-backed sleep/calm supplements; glycinate costs a little more than oxide but is worth it for absorption and tolerability.

dosing.note: Typical supplemental dose is **200-400 mg elemental magnesium**. The 350 mg tolerable upper limit applies to supplements only (not food) and exists because excess causes diarrhoea. Doses are given as elemental magnesium, which is a fraction of the salt weight (e.g. glycinate is ~14% elemental).

timing.line: For sleep and calm, take **glycinate 30-60 minutes before bed**; malate or citrate are better earlier in the day if you want it for muscle/recovery.

cycling.line: **No cycling needed** — magnesium is an essential mineral you use daily, so continuous use to maintain status is appropriate.

contra.advice: Impaired kidneys clear magnesium poorly, so it can accumulate to dangerous levels — do not supplement without medical supervision if you have reduced renal function.
Magnesium binds and reduces absorption of tetracycline/quinolone antibiotics, bisphosphonates and thyroid hormone — separate doses by at least 2-4 hours.
If you get diarrhoea, lower the dose or switch from citrate/oxide to glycinate rather than pushing through.

quality.line: Choose a product that **lists elemental magnesium** and is **third-party tested** (e.g. NSF or USP verified); avoid proprietary blends that hide the form and dose.

nonResponders.line: If you are already magnesium-replete, the sleep/calm effect is modest — the biggest responders are people who are depleted (hard training, high stress, alcohol, or an oxide-only supplement they weren't absorbing).

synergy.why: The glycinate form itself delivers glycine, an inhibitory neurotransmitter with its own mild sleep-quality benefit — part of why this form is favoured at night.
Magnesium is needed to activate vitamin D and vice versa; correcting both together tends to work better than either alone.
B6 may aid cellular magnesium uptake and the pair is commonly combined for stress/PMS-related calm, though the uptake evidence is limited.

form.buy: Pure **L-theanine** (the single L-isomer), third-party tested for identity and purity, with the actual L-theanine content stated in milligrams rather than hidden inside a vague 'tea extract' blend.
form.avoid: **Racemic DL-theanine** and unstandardised green-tea extracts that don't declare actual theanine milligrams — both are cheaper but under-studied at the doses that matter.
form.withFood: No food needed; absorption is efficient and largely unaffected by whether the stomach is empty or full.
form.bioavailability: **Rapidly absorbed** and crosses the blood-brain barrier; plasma peaks around 30-50 minutes with a short half-life (~1 hour), which is why effects are felt quickly but fade within a few hours.

foodFirst.line: **Green tea** is the natural source, but a typical cup delivers only roughly 8-25 mg of L-theanine — high-grade shade-grown leaf (gyokuro, matcha) is richer (~25-45 mg) but still far below a 100-200 mg supplemental dose.
foodFirst.note: You'd need many cups to reach studied doses, and that caffeine load defeats the purpose for a calm/sleep goal — this is a case where a capsule is the practical route.

cost.math: A bottle of 60-120 capsules (100-200 mg each) runs roughly S$15-30, so per-dose cost is low even used daily.
cost.note: One of the cheaper foundational supplements; no need to overpay for premium framing.

dosing.cap: Commonly studied up to ~400 mg per dose; there's no reason to exceed ~400 mg for everyday use.
dosing.note: Typical effective range is **100-200 mg**. For focus, pair with caffeine; for calm or sleep, use it alone. Per-kg dosing is not standard for this compound.

timing.line: For **smooth focus**, take alongside caffeine (a ~2:1 theanine:caffeine ratio, e.g. 200 mg theanine : 100 mg caffeine, is the classic combo). For **calm or sleep**, take 100-200 mg on its own, 30-60 minutes before you want the effect.

cycling.line: **No cycling needed** — L-theanine shows no known tolerance, dependence, or withdrawal, so daily use is fine. It also doesn't build up; each dose acts and clears within hours.

contra.advice: L-theanine may modestly blunt stress-related blood-pressure rises; evidence for lowering resting blood pressure is limited, but if you're on antihypertensives it's worth mentioning to your doctor.
Safety of concentrated supplemental doses hasn't been established — food-level green tea is one thing, 200 mg capsules another; default to omitting.
Generally well tolerated, but since theanine blunts stimulant 'edge', don't assume your usual stim dose feels the same — introduce it separately so you can tell what's doing what.

quality.line: Choose products that are **third-party tested** and state the L-isomer explicitly; independent standards such as NSF Certified for Sport or Informed Sport are useful neutral assurance if you compete and need a clean-label guarantee.

nonResponders.line: A meaningful minority feel **little subjective effect**, especially taken alone without caffeine — the caffeine-pairing benefit (less jitter, steadier attention) is the most reliably noticed effect. If nothing registers after a couple of weeks, it may simply be a non-responder situation, not a dosing problem.

synergy.why: The best-supported combination: L-theanine takes the jittery, wired edge off caffeine while attention and reaction-time benefits are preserved or improved — smooth focus instead of a buzz.
Both support a calmer pre-sleep state through different mechanisms; a reasonable emerging stack for evening wind-down, though evidence for the combination specifically is thin.

form.buy: **Root extract standardized to withanolides** (typically 2.5-5%). This is the form used in nearly all clinical trials, so the active dose is knowable.
form.avoid: **Non-standardized raw root powder (churna)** with unlisted withanolide content, and cheap products that don't publish a withanolide %.
form.withFood: Withanolides are **fat-soluble**; taking with a meal (or the traditional warm milk) aids absorption and reduces occasional GI upset. Not mandatory.
form.bioavailability: Effects track the **withanolide dose**, not the raw milligrams, so two products at the same mg can differ severalfold in potency.

biomarkers.why: Primary mechanism is turning down the **HPA-axis / cortisol** set point; RCTs and meta-analyses show real reductions, though a biological cortisol drop does not always track subjective stress relief.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and again at 8 weeks, morning and fasted

biomarkers.why: Because chronic stress suppresses T, lowering cortisol can produce a **modest T rise** in stressed or subfertile men; not an effect to expect in already-optimized men.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and 8-12 weeks, morning

biomarkers.why: Ashwagandha may nudge **thyroid hormones up and TSH down**, but the human data is essentially one trial in subclinical-hypothyroid patients (no change seen in healthy people) — track mainly as a safety check if you have thyroid issues.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and 8-12 weeks if thyroid status is a concern

foodFirst.line: No food contains ashwagandha; the **cortisol/sleep/testosterone axis it targets responds first to sleep, resistance training, and reduced alcohol** — fix those before or alongside supplementing.
foodFirst.note: Ashwagandha is a lever on stress physiology, not a substitute for the behaviors that set it.

cost.math: A month of standardized root-extract capsules (600 mg/day) typically runs ~SGD 15-35 in Singapore; pharmacy tins of 60 caps sit around SGD 24-32, with online importers cheaper.
cost.note: Standardized extracts cost more than plain powder but are what the evidence is built on.

dosing.cap: 600 mg/day covers essentially all positive trials; more is not better-supported
dosing.note: **300-600 mg/day** of extract standardized to ~5% withanolides is the clinical range; can be a single dose or split AM/PM.

timing.line: Any time works; if the goal is **sleep and evening calm, take it with dinner or before bed**. Splitting AM/PM suits general daytime stress.

cycling.line: Most trials ran **8-12 weeks**, so long-term safety data is limited; some users cycle (e.g. 8-12 weeks on, a short break) though evidence for needing to cycle is thin.

contra.advice: Avoid — traditionally regarded as an abortifacient and not established as safe.
Can raise thyroid hormones; avoid or monitor TSH/T4 closely and involve your doctor.

contra.flag: Liver disease or other hepatotoxic drugs
contra.advice: Rare cases of ashwagandha-associated liver injury are documented; stop immediately if you notice jaundice, dark urine, or right-upper-abdomen pain.
Additive sedation is possible; be cautious combining, and stop ~2 weeks before scheduled surgery.
May stimulate immune activity in theory; discuss with your clinician before use.

quality.line: Choose products that publish a **withanolide %** and are **third-party tested for heavy metals** — Ayurvedic root herbs can carry lead/arsenic contamination. Independent certification (e.g. NSF, USP) is a plus.

nonResponders.line: Effects on stress and sleep are usually subtle; if you notice **no change in sleep, calm, or stress by ~8 weeks** at 600 mg/day, it likely isn't doing much for you.

synergy.why: Complementary support for **sleep quality and evening relaxation**, though the combination hasn't been formally trialed.
Another well-tolerated **calming** agent that pairs logically for daytime stress; combination evidence is limited.

form.buy: **Berberine HCl** is the standard, well-studied form; **dihydroberberine** is a more bioavailable derivative that allows lower doses. Choose high-purity, third-party-tested capsules.
form.avoid: Cheap **blends** that hide the actual berberine dose inside a proprietary matrix, and any product without a stated mg content per capsule.
form.withFood: Take **with or just before a meal** — food improves tolerance and lets it blunt the post-meal glucose rise.
form.bioavailability: Oral bioavailability is **very low (<1%)**, from extensive intestinal first-pass metabolism, poor solubility, and gut P-glycoprotein efflux; this is why dosing is split and dihydroberberine or absorption enhancers are used.

biomarkers.why: Primary target — berberine's AMPK effect lowers fasting and post-meal glucose.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then 8-12 weeks

biomarkers.why: Best 3-month average of glycemic control; trials show meaningful reductions in dysglycemic people.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then every 3 months

biomarkers.why: Berberine reliably lowers triglycerides — a core reason to use it.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then 8-12 weeks

biomarkers.why: Modest LDL-lowering via upregulated LDL-receptor expression.
biomarkers.range: General target <3.0 mmol/L (lower if higher CV risk)
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then 8-12 weeks

biomarkers.why: Precautionary check for a long-term add-on supplement cleared by the liver; berberine is not a known hepatotoxin, so this is prudence rather than an established need.
biomarkers.when: Baseline and if using long-term

foodFirst.line: Berberine has **no meaningful dietary source** — it is an isolated plant alkaloid, not a nutrient you can eat your way to.
foodFirst.note: The glucose and triglyceride levers it targets respond first to fiber, reduced refined carbs, and post-meal walking; berberine is an add-on to that base, not a replacement.

cost.math: A bottle of 60-90 high-purity 500 mg caps runs roughly SGD $18-35, so a 1000-1500 mg/day protocol is about $0.40-1.50/day.
cost.note: Dihydroberberine costs notably more per equivalent effect.

dosing.note: Standard is **500 mg taken 2-3x daily with meals** (1000-1500 mg/day). Start with a single daily dose and build up to assess GI tolerance. Per-kg dosing is not used for berberine.

timing.line: Split across the day and take **with or ~15-20 min before carb-containing meals** — its glucose-blunting effect and low plasma levels make single large doses both harsher on the gut and less useful.

cycling.line: Some users **cycle** (e.g. 8-12 weeks on, a short break) partly over gut-microbiome concerns, but there is no strong evidence that cycling is necessary — this is emerging/anecdotal.

contra.advice: Avoid — berberine crosses the placenta and can displace bilirubin, posing a kernicterus risk to the fetus/newborn. Do not give to infants.
Berberine inhibits CYP3A4/CYP2D6 and P-glycoprotein, raising blood levels of many drugs (some statins, cyclosporine, and others). Space dosing and clear it with a pharmacist/doctor before combining.
Additive effect can cause hypoglycemia — monitor closely and involve your prescriber rather than stacking blindly.
Cramping, diarrhea or constipation are common early on — start with one 500 mg dose with food and titrate up.

quality.line: Choose products **third-party tested** for actual berberine content and for heavy-metal/contaminant limits; botanical extracts vary widely and cheap material can be adulterated or under-dosed.

nonResponders.line: Response varies partly because **gut bacteria (via nitroreductase) convert berberine to the better-absorbed dihydroberberine** — differing microbiomes (and heavy P-glycoprotein efflux) mean some people absorb far less; switching to dihydroberberine can help.

synergy.why: Better absorbed than berberine HCl, so a lower dose achieves similar glucose/lipid effects with less GI upset.
Inhibits P-glycoprotein efflux, which can raise berberine's low absorption — a mechanistically sound but still emerging pairing.
Berberine's AMPK/glucose-disposal effect stacks with the same lever pulled by lower refined-carb intake and light post-meal activity.

form.buy: **Well-absorbed chelates** — zinc picolinate, citrate, glycinate/bisglycinate, or gluconate. Look for the *elemental* zinc amount on the label, not the salt weight.
form.avoid: **Zinc oxide** — cheap and common in multivitamins but poorly absorbed. Also avoid mega-dose (50 mg+) single tablets for daily use.
form.withFood: Take **with a meal** to prevent nausea. Empty-stomach zinc is the classic cause of that queasy, metallic gut-punch feeling.
form.bioavailability: Absorption is blunted by **phytates** (whole grains, legumes) and by co-ingested calcium and iron; this applies across all forms.

biomarkers.why: The standard lab, but an **imperfect marker** — it drops in inflammation and doesn't capture tissue stores. Useful for confirming frank deficiency, weak for fine-tuning.
biomarkers.when: Fasting morning draw; recheck 8–12 weeks after starting

biomarkers.why: **The one to actually watch.** Sustained zinc supplementation drives copper down; low copper causes anemia and neuropathy. Track it if you dose above ~25 mg/day for months.
biomarkers.when: Baseline, then every 3–6 months on ongoing higher-dose zinc

biomarkers.why: Only relevant to the T goal, and only informative if you were **deficient first** — correcting low zinc can restore T, but topping up a replete man does nothing.
biomarkers.when: Baseline before starting if T is your reason for using it

depletes.role: High zinc induces intestinal metallothionein, which traps dietary copper — the best-documented downside of chronic supplementation.
Zinc and non-heme iron compete for the same absorption transporters; large zinc doses can lower iron uptake.

antagonists.role: Co-ingested calcium (dairy, supplements) reduces zinc absorption — separate them by a couple of hours.
Bind zinc in the gut; a plant-heavy, high-legume/grain diet meaningfully lowers absorbed zinc.

foodFirst.line: **Oysters** are the outlier — a few cover a full day's zinc many times over. Red meat, shellfish, poultry, and pumpkin seeds are reliable everyday sources.
foodFirst.note: Animal-source zinc is far better absorbed than plant zinc because it isn't locked up by phytates.

cost.math: A bottle of 100+ tablets typically runs S$8–15, i.e. cents per day.
cost.note: One of the cheapest supplements in existence — cost is never the limiting factor.

dosing.note: Everyday maintenance sits around **15–30 mg elemental**. Short high-dose immune protocols (e.g. lozenges at cold onset) run higher but are time-limited, not daily. Chronic dosing near or above 40 mg risks copper deficiency.

timing.line: With a meal to avoid nausea, but **separate by 2+ hours from iron or calcium supplements** and from tetracycline/quinolone antibiotics, which zinc binds and inactivates.

cycling.line: No formal cycle needed at maintenance doses, but **higher-dose zinc should not run indefinitely** — use it to correct a deficiency or for time-limited immune support, then drop back to food-level or a modest maintenance dose to protect copper.

contra.advice: Chronic intake above ~40 mg/day can cause **copper-deficiency anemia and neuropathy** that may not fully reverse. Pair higher doses with 1–2 mg copper and monitor labs.
Zinc chelates **tetracyclines and fluoroquinolones**, reducing their absorption. Separate doses by several hours.
Common on an empty stomach or at high single doses. Take with food and split larger amounts.

quality.line: Zinc is simple and rarely adulterated; choose a **third-party tested** product (USP or NSF verified) and confirm the label states elemental zinc, not just the salt weight.

nonResponders.line: If your zinc status is already normal, **extra zinc does nothing** — it won't raise testosterone, immunity, or recovery above baseline. The benefit is deficiency correction, not enhancement.

synergy.why: Not a performance stack — a **safety pairing**. Adding a little copper offsets the depletion that higher-dose zinc causes.
Commonly combined for sleep and recovery; benefits are most convincing when one or more of the nutrients was low to begin with.

form.buy: **Hydrolyzed collagen peptides** (unflavored) — cleaved into di/tripeptides that dissolve fully in cold liquid and survive digestion better than intact gelatin.
form.avoid: Skip pricey **marine vs bovine** marketing claims — for connective-tissue support the amino acid profile (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline) is what matters, not the source animal.
form.withFood: Take on a relatively empty stomach with a small carb/liquid vehicle ~45-60 min pre-training so the amino acid spike lands during loading; a large mixed meal blunts and delays the peak.
form.bioavailability: Peptides like **prolyl-hydroxyproline** appear intact in blood within ~1 hr and transiently raise glycine/proline substrate for collagen synthesis.

needs.role: Essential cofactor for **prolyl and lysyl hydroxylase**, the enzymes that hydroxylate and stabilize the collagen triple helix — the classic protocol pairs ~50 mg with each dose.
Cofactor for **lysyl oxidase**, which cross-links mature collagen fibrils for tensile strength.

foodFirst.line: **Bone broth, gelatin, and skin/connective-tissue cuts** deliver the same glycine/proline/hydroxyproline pool.
foodFirst.note: Concentration is inconsistent and it's hard to hit ~15 g of collagen-specific amino acids reliably in the pre-training window without a measured peptide dose.

cost.math: Unflavored 500 g tub ≈ $30–45 SGD ÷ ~33 doses of 15 g (≈ $0.90–1.40/dose)
cost.note: Cheapest as bulk unflavored powder; flavored/blended tubs cost noticeably more per gram of actual collagen.

dosing.note: Controlled loading studies (gelatin + vitamin C) used 5–15 g; 15 g roughly doubled the collagen-synthesis marker (P1NP) versus placebo, with 5 g giving an intermediate response. Doses above ~15 g haven't been shown to add benefit for this purpose.

timing.line: Take **~45–60 min before loading** (jumps, sprints, resistance work, or targeted tendon/ligament exercise) so the amino acid peak coincides with mechanical stress on the tissue.

cycling.line: No cycling needed — benefit accrues from **consistent pre-load dosing over weeks to months**, not from a saturating dose, so daily/training-day use is the point.

contra.advice: Avoid **marine-sourced collagen**; choose bovine or porcine peptides instead.
Keep the paired vitamin C modest (~50 mg, not gram doses) since high-dose ascorbate can raise urinary **oxalate**.
Collagen is a protein load — count it toward your daily protein target and clear extra supplemental protein with your clinician.

quality.line: Choose **third-party-tested, heavy-metal-screened** peptides — collagen extracted from hide/bone can concentrate lead and cadmium; NSF or Informed Sport certification matters if you compete.

nonResponders.line: Individual variation in the collagen-synthesis response exists, and the tissue effect is slow — judge over **8–12+ weeks** of consistent pre-load dosing, not days.

synergy.why: Rate-limiting cofactor for helix hydroxylation — without adequate ascorbate the collagen you're feeding can't be properly assembled.
The pre-training timing exists because **intermittent loading** is the actual stimulus that drives amino acids into new tendon/ligament collagen; the peptides just supply substrate.

form.buy: **Standardized extract** to ~3% rosavins and ~1% salidroside (the roughly **3:1 ratio** found in the root and used in most human trials); look for third-party-tested capsules or tablets.
form.avoid: Raw/unstandardized root powder and products listing only 'Rhodiola extract' with no rosavin/salidroside percentage; potency is unpredictable.
form.withFood: Take in the **morning, on an empty stomach** — this is the convention used in trials and keeps dosing early enough to avoid sleep disruption; food is not required.
form.bioavailability: Salidroside and rosavins are absorbed reasonably well orally; the active constituents are what matter, so standardization is the real lever, not formulation.

biomarkers.why: Rhodiola is studied for blunting the stress-induced cortisol rise; a morning salivary panel can give a rough personal signal of stress-axis response.
biomarkers.range: Lab-dependent; interpret change over weeks against your own baseline rather than a fixed cutoff.
biomarkers.when: Waking and ~30 min post-waking, before and after 4-8 weeks of use.

foodFirst.line: No dietary source exists — Rhodiola is a herb, not a nutrient. **Sleep, aerobic base fitness, and load management** address the same stress-linked fatigue Rhodiola nudges and should be fixed first.
foodFirst.note: Treat it as an adjunct for stress-linked fatigue, not a substitute for recovery fundamentals.

cost.math: A bottle of 60 standardized capsules (~S$20-35) at one capsule/day.
cost.note: Cheap unstandardized powder costs less but often under-delivers active constituents — false economy.

dosing.note: Chronic anti-fatigue dose is typically **200-400 mg/day**; trials for stress-related fatigue and depression have used up to ~576-680 mg/day. For an acute cognitive/stress task, a single **200-400 mg** dose ~30-60 min before. Onset for chronic fatigue is gradual over 1-2 weeks. Per-kg dosing is not standard.

timing.line: **Morning, empty stomach.** It is mildly stimulating — dosing in the afternoon or evening can disrupt sleep.

cycling.line: Commonly **cycled** (e.g. 6-8 weeks on, 1-2 weeks off) to limit tolerance, though rigorous data on whether cycling is necessary is thin.

contra.advice: Its activating, mood-lifting effect could theoretically trigger agitation or hypomania — avoid unless cleared by your doctor.
Has documented MAO-inhibiting and serotonergic activity in lab studies and may stack with caffeine/other stimulants — be cautious combining with SSRIs/SNRIs/MAOIs and discuss with a clinician.
Insufficient safety data — avoid.
The stimulation can feel like overstimulation or a racing feeling; start at the low end (100-200 mg) and assess.

quality.line: **Species adulteration is common** — cheaper *Rhodiola crenulata* is often sold as *R. rosea*. Insist on labelled *Rhodiola rosea* with stated rosavin/salidroside percentages and third-party testing.

nonResponders.line: A meaningful minority feel **nothing**, while others feel **over-stimulated or jittery** rather than energised; if two weeks at a proper standardized dose does nothing, it is likely a non-response.

synergy.why: Both reduce fatigue and sharpen alertness; a low caffeine dose can complement Rhodiola, but stacking full doses risks jitteriness and overstimulation.
Different profile — Rhodiola is activating/morning, ashwagandha is calming/evening; some pair them across the day for stress without a sedation-vs-stimulation conflict.

form.buy: **High-purity crystalline glycine powder** — naturally sweet, dissolves clear in water or tea; capsules exist but powder is far cheaper for the same molecule.
form.withFood: Fine on an empty stomach; for sleep take it shortly before bed. No food needed for absorption.
form.bioavailability: **Rapidly and nearly completely absorbed** orally as a free amino acid.

needs.role: Cofactor for the prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase enzymes that build collagen — the connective-tissue benefit of glycine depends on adequate vitamin C.
Co-substrate with glycine and glutamate for **glutathione** synthesis; glycine is frequently the rate-limiting brick.

foodFirst.line: **Bone broth, gelatin, and collagen-rich cuts** (skin, tendon, slow-cooked connective tissue) are naturally loaded with glycine.
foodFirst.note: Muscle-meat-heavy modern diets skew relatively low in glycine because the connective tissue that carries it is often discarded — this is the main gap supplementation fills.

cost.math: ~$30-40 SGD/kg bulk powder ÷ ~330 doses of 3 g
cost.note: One of the cheapest supplements in existence; bulk powder is dramatically cheaper than capsules for the identical compound.

dosing.cap: Typical self-use 3-5 g; research doses of ~15-30 g/day (and higher in schizophrenia trials) have been used safely short-term.
dosing.note: **3 g before bed** is the dose used in the sleep trials; larger amounts are used for collagen/glutathione goals. Start low — high single doses can cause mild GI upset.

timing.line: For sleep, take **~3 g about 30-60 minutes before bed**. For connective-tissue or glutathione goals timing is irrelevant — take any time, ideally alongside vitamin C for collagen.

cycling.line: **No cycling needed** — glycine is a dietary amino acid safe for continuous daily use, with no known tolerance or dependence.

contra.advice: Placebo-controlled trials suggest glycine can **blunt clozapine's antipsychotic effect** — do not supplement without psychiatric guidance.
Clear high-dose amino-acid supplementation with your doctor first, as impaired nitrogen/protein handling changes the calculus.

quality.line: Choose **pharmaceutical / USP-grade crystalline glycine**, third-party tested for heavy metals and purity; it should taste faintly sweet and dissolve to a clear solution.

nonResponders.line: Not everyone feels a **sleep or calming effect** — but the connective-tissue and glutathione-substrate roles are structural biochemistry and don't depend on a noticeable subjective response.

synergy.why: Supplies both limiting substrates for **glutathione**, the body's master antioxidant; the combination has been studied in aging and metabolic health.
Required cofactor for **collagen cross-linking** — pairs naturally with glycine for skin and connective-tissue support.
Both are calming to the nervous system and are commonly stacked for **sleep and relaxation**, though the combined benefit is more anecdotal than proven.

form.buy: Both **pure L-citrulline** and **citrulline malate** (citrulline bound to malic acid) are effective; choose high-purity, third-party-tested amino-acid powder.
form.avoid: **L-arginine as a substitute** — oral arginine is largely destroyed by intestinal and hepatic arginase, so it raises plasma arginine less reliably than citrulline.
form.withFood: Works either way; an **empty stomach** speeds absorption but is not required for benefit.
form.bioavailability: Citrulline **bypasses first-pass arginase**, so gram-for-gram it raises plasma arginine and nitric-oxide output more than oral arginine itself.

biomarkers.why: Primary trackable outcome — citrulline modestly lowers resting BP (meta-analyses ~4/2.5 mmHg) via nitric-oxide vasodilation, with larger effects in people with elevated baseline readings.
biomarkers.when: Home cuff, seated, mornings; compare over 4-8 weeks

needs.role: Essential NOS cofactor for converting arginine into nitric oxide; when depleted the enzyme uncouples
Helps recycle and stabilise BH4, supporting nitric-oxide production

foodFirst.line: **Watermelon** is the richest common dietary source of citrulline (found mainly in the flesh, with the rind also contributing).
foodFirst.note: Flesh holds only ~3 g citrulline per kg, so you'd need roughly 2 kg to reach a performance dose — food is a top-up, not a replacement for supplemental dosing.

cost.math: Bulk citrulline-malate powder ~S$25-35 per 250 g ≈ S$0.10-0.14/g
cost.note: Powder is far cheaper than capsules; malate needs a larger scoop than pure citrulline for the same citrulline content.

dosing.cap: Above ~10 g adds little and may cause mild GI discomfort.
dosing.note: **L-citrulline 3-6 g/day** for blood pressure; **6-8 g L-citrulline (or ~8 g citrulline malate)** pre-exercise for pump and endurance. Per-kg dosing is not standard.

timing.line: For pump and performance take **~60 minutes before training**; for blood pressure, dose **daily** — the vascular effect builds over days to weeks, not from a single dose.

cycling.line: **No cycling needed** — citrulline does not cause tolerance or receptor downregulation.

contra.flag: On nitrates or PDE5 inhibitors (e.g. sildenafil)
contra.advice: Additive vasodilation can drop blood pressure too far — do not combine without medical guidance.
May add to the BP-lowering effect; monitor readings and discuss with your doctor.

quality.line: Choose amino-acid powder that is **third-party tested** (e.g. NSF, Informed Sport); for malate, confirm the **stated malate ratio** (commonly 2:1 or 1:1) so you know the actual citrulline content.

nonResponders.line: **Pump is subjective and variable**, and performance gains are modest; blood-pressure and endurance effects are more consistently measurable. GI tolerance is good — citrulline is gentler on the gut than arginine.

synergy.why: Raises nitric oxide through a separate, NOS-independent pathway — complementary vasodilation and endurance benefit.
Co-ingestion can raise plasma arginine more than either alone by pairing acute delivery with sustained conversion.
May help sustain nitric oxide by protecting it from oxidative breakdown.
